sentences.text: Well, my greatest fears were to live through and not to be caught by the Germans and be-- you know, being killed.
That-- that was-- that was really the main thing, to survive.
And I was telling myself all along at that time, well, when I survive, I'm going to buy myself a cow.
And I'm going to take it to the pasture so I can milk it, and I can have as much milk and as much sour cream as I really want.
Well, that was not the end yet.
Because finally, after we moved out from this place-- which I don't know where it was-- we went somehow to this place, Mochulyanka.
That was our final-- the last-- the last place.
We found there, again-- again, my mother-- I-- I know what happened, I think, now.
While we were in this-- in the forest, my mother met this man named Goldman who was my father's good friend, and who was really after my mother.
And he lived with us in-- in Ludvipol, in the ghetto.
And he told my mother that he and some other people are hidden, or staying with this Polish family, Korkus.
And if my mother wants to, he will make arrangements so we can stay with them, too.
And this was really wonderful, because they were very nice people.
I mean, again, we slept on those-- all over the place, and as I said, and if you got that spot on the oven, it was great.
And it was a very nice family.
We became really very friendly with them.
They were very nice to us.
I'm sure that my mother paid something.
And I learned how to knit, and I was making socks for them, wool socks.
And I was helping them with [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] linen-- what do you call it, the machine?
Weaving.
With the weaving.
And I was helping them in the house.
They had children, so I was helping them with the children.
And I was helping them with whatever the was around the house.
But I-- I guess my mother must have, too.
I don't remember.
And we stayed with them, and there were some other Jews, too, at that time.
Probably, yes.
I mean, everybody was-- the only room that-- these-- these-- this was an old couple with two daughters.
One of the daughter was married.
So the only-- so this newly married couple had a room to themselves.
Otherwise, everybody was all together.
There was-- There was no privacy whatsoever.
Everybody slept wherever there was, or ate at the big-- the round wooden table.
There was, I remember, a big-- but that was wonderful already.
There was a big wooden bowl put on the table with boiled potatoes, and another wooden bowl with sour cream.
And the potatoes we would take with our hands out of that bowl, and the sour cream-- not sour cream, sour milk.
And the sour milk was, I guess, in some kind of a-- there had to be a spoon for it already because you couldn't eat with your hands.
But that was wonderful, because we had boiled potatoes, and we had sour cream-- sour-- sour milk with it.
Well, they gave the food-- I mean, the people.
Yes.
They ate with us.
Everybody ate all together at that time from that one big bowl.
Who prepared, I don't remember.
Probably, you know, the women that were there helped the-- the woman of the house.
Yes.
They were concerned.
But their house, I think, was a little further out of the village.
It was not in the middle of the village.
And they were probably paid by this Mr.
Goldman.
My mother probably paid, too.
But yes, they did take a risk.
No question about it.

So now, again, when there was fear or there was rumor that the Germans were approaching or coming, we all evacuated, left the house, and went to that-- to the in-ground-- in-ground shelter because that was in the closer proximity to their-- to their village or to their house.
Was that shelter or any of the other shelters-- did the Germans or the Ukrainians ever raid any of them?
No, no.
The Germans didn't because, as I said, the Germans were afraid.
And somehow, I guess the villages that we stayed around-- they were more-- more Polish than Ukrainian.
And if there were a few Ukrainians there, they didn't-- you know, they-- they didn't want to be-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they wanted to be iso-- they didn't want to be isolated.
They wanted, really, to be integrated in the Polish community rather than showing that-- further east, where my husband comes from, there was more of the Ukrainian [UKRAINIAN] and [UKRAINIAN].
And they were-- they were merciless.
Yes.
While we were there already, we started-- not so much from the west.
We didn't know, again-- I mean, I wasn't aware of what was happening in Poland.
As a matter of fact, we really didn't know what was going-- we had no idea what happened to our family.
I don't remember if my mother had gotten some news or not.
But I-- I was not aware of what-- what was going on.
But yes, we were aware that the front was coming closer, and sometimes we heard the Front was already there, and we were getting all excited.
And then we heard the [? cartouches ?] and then, quiet again.
And again, the Front wasn't coming.
So this went on for quite a few months with our anticipation of being liberated, and of the war to be over.
By then, we knew that the German were, I think, almost finished with, and that the Russians were coming.
And we were waiting for the Russians, naturally, needless to say, like our saviors.
Yeah.
Were you aware of the concentration camps--
No.
As I said, I wasn't.
And I don't know what-- I don't know what happened-- you know, what happened.
I mean, if my mother knew, I don't know.
I didn't know.
Probably from the partisans, because at that time, I think that started some kind of a connection with the-- with the partisans.

And I guess some of the Jews probably entered the-- the partisans and made-- there must have been some kind of a connection with-- with the people, either with the village people or with our Jewish people that were staying in the house.
No, I was-- I was a little girl.
No, I-- I didn't.
I'm not sure if my uncle, the one that survived, whether he wasn't a part of it.
But again, I don't remember.
No.
Not that I'm-- not that I'm aware of.
No.
No.
Oh, yeah, there were a lot of-- very few people survived from typhus.
Very few people.
I mean, whoever got-- and there was, like, an epidemic at that time.
Whoever got typhus, I mean, died.
Why my mother survived, I don't know.
Maybe her stamina was-- you know what?
It was [NON-ENGLISH].
I don't know.
Well, we were in such a big group of people, I'm sure there were.
But, you know, we weren't with such a large group of people.
So I don't know if any-- I mean, in my group that survived, that I was with-- whether-- I mean, I know that this man was, yes, very, you know, very much after my mother.
He really liked-- and especially after my father was dead.
I mean, there was my mother, I mean, a young woman whom he liked all along, and he was very much after her.
No.
I guess I became very old very fast.
I matured very fast.
And I guess whatever I communicated, I communicated with whoever was there, with the adults.
I didn't have my-- my young days were gone.
The Russians.
Well, I guess.
I remember that somebody came home, I think, and said, the Russians are there.
And we-- Mochulyanka.
Yes.
And we all went wild.
We all went wild.
We were screaming.
We were yelling.
We were-- we were happy.
We were crying.
We were-- all the emotions, I think, that were pent up all-- you know, the whole year, all burst.

And the first thing that I did-- well, maybe not the immediate first thing, but one of the very first things-- I remember I was 12 years old-- I asked somebody to take me to the-- to the grave, to the men's grave.
[CRYING] And I remember like today that I was sitting there at that grave, and I was crying.
I was crying bitterly.
These, I think, were the first tears that came.
And I'm crying even today.
It's interesting.
[CRYING] I loved my father very much.
And he loved me tremendously.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And unfortunately, we didn't have many years together.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Mochulyanka, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we remained, I think probably from-- I don't know.
A few-- I don't remember-- a month, two months.
Yes.
And then--
Well-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember.
We had no problems.
I mean, I don't remember any problem.
And then my mother started looking for something to do because, you know, the war was over, and, I mean, she had two little children for whom she had to provide.
So she found out that, in the next little town, which was called Stara Huta, there was a hospital.
So she went to that hospital and got a job as a con-- not concierge, as a food buyer or-- what do you call it?
Like a host-- not hostess.
In the hotels, what do you call-- concierge.
Something of this sort.
Well, she got a job there.
And I think we moved to Stara Huta.
She found some kind of a room in somebody's house.
We moved there, and while she was working in this hospital, she met my stepfather, who was a doctor there.
The picture is there.
And--
No, he was from Poland.
He was from Lodz.
And I don't know whether they got married right away.
I mean, at that time, you said, yes, they got married.
I mean, I don't think they got married right away, then, because there was no one even to-- whatever.
Anyhow, they remained.
Eventually they got married.
And we were in Stara Huta until the war ended, until 1945, after the war ended.
Yes.
That's right.
For a year, we were there.
And we were very close to die there, because all of a sudden, one night, the Bandera, the Ukrainian group, came at night.
And I think, in that hospital, there was a wounded prisoner of theirs.
And they wanted to get that prisoner out, because they were afraid that he was going to be executed by the Russians.
And in order to get into that hospital, they started shooting, and they killed quite a few people.
And we were lucky because we-- at that time, I think we lived in the hospital.
And they got him out, and thank God.
You know, we were, I think, on the top floor someplace, so they didn't get there.
And we stayed there until-- I think I went to school there because we went to school.
And you know, when the Russians came, they started with the propaganda.
And they started the music in the parks, and they started dancing in the parks.
And I remember, I mean-- I think I was going there and see the young people, you know, the Jews and whatever, dancing, having-- trying to get back-- trying to get back to life.
And I think my uncle was-- maybe that was before the start of-- my uncle was with us.
I remember we slept on the floor in things like-- like herrings.
And when one turned, everybody had to turn.
I think this was before we moved to Stara Huta.
That was still in Mochulyanka.
And so life started going back to some kind-- you know, having some kind of order.
I started going back to Russian school.
And we were there until-- until the war ended, as I said before.
Well, we didn't have any until we got back to Lodz, to Poland.
And that was in 1945.
It had to be in the summer of 1945, when they started to have transports out of the-- when-- when there was the pact between Germany and Russia.
And Germany was giving up their terrains on the west part of Poland, and they took the terrains on the eastern part of Poland, like Lvov and all the thing.
The Russians started to have transports of-- whoever wanted to immigrate to-- you know, out of there to Poland, they could.
So we hopped on one of those transports and got to Lodz, not having any idea of what's going-- of what we are going to find or whatever.
And while on this one, we got off the train.
And the ride took two weeks to get back, two weeks in-- this was in cattle-- in cattle trains that we were going.
And when my mother got off the train, all of a sudden, she met somebody.
She says, oh, do you know that your-- your husband's sister is-- lives-- lived through the war, is in Lodz right now, and she is there with her two daughters.
So that's how we reunited with part of the family, stayed in Lodz for about half a year.
And my stepfather at that time started looking for something to do.
And Lodz was a little hard to get into, you know, to get a job or practice or whatever.
So everybody was going, again, west, to the Western parts-- the Western part that was German before.
So the two of them went and ended up in Legnica, found an apartment there.
And people just came in and took apartments.
They were throwing out the Germans, taking everything, whatever was the Germans', and you just settled down.
That's it.
Where is Legnica?
Legnica is on the Western part of Poland.
It's near Broslev, Bratslav, whatever have you heard.
It was the-- Legnica had a big Russian army there for many, many years.
Rokossovsky was stationed with his big army in Legnica.
No, that was Poland.
No, but after the war.
Well, it was Poland.
Yeah.
I mean, the Russian army was all over the place, was in Poland, was in, you know, in all the eastern countries.
But it was called the Common Polish Communist Government.
So after they found their apartment, and whatever, they came back and took me and my sister.
In the meantime, I was going to school in Lodz for just maybe a couple of months or half a year.
I went to the sixth grade.
Who'd they leave you with?
Well, my mother took an apartment, a small apartment-- a room, really, in somebody's house.
And we were a little bit with my aunt and my cousin-- my cousins.
And then we took the apartment-- my mother took the apartment, so we stayed there.
And they went east.
Well, listen.
At that time, I was, what, 14 years old?
And at that time, I was a grown-- a grown person.

I was very mature, you know, at that time already after going through-- as a matter of fact, one-- during one of the times when my mother went to-- went west to look for an apartment, I just went, got on the train.
I was hanging on the train, really, because at that time, that was right after the war, and the schedules of the trains were terrible.
And it was a mess, a mess.
You couldn't get into the train.
That's how many people traveled-- on the roof, on the-- I mean, it was unbelievable.
I by myself went to Radom because I found out also from my aunt that my aunt survived in Radom.
And I wanted very much-- I remembered her as a single woman who used to take me on her dates, and who bought me my first ice skates.
So I was very attached to her.
And having somebody, you know, that survived, I had to see her.
So all by myself, I went.
It took me one night to get back.
It took me one day to be in Radom and one day-- a whole day to get back to Lodz.
I made it.
Did you find her?
Yeah.
Yeah.
I found her.
We spent one day, and I went back.
Well, at that time, when we got to Lodz, we found from my aunt that everybody was killed because they knew-- they knew everything, whatever.
Because my aunt was-- I think my aunt was on Polish papers, too, but I'm not sure.
My aunt might have been in a camp.
But my cousins were-- one of my cousins was saved in Warsaw under a Polish name, and worked as a Polish maid.
So from them, we found out about the concentration camps, and found out about the families, and found out who-- you know, that everybody was killed.
We also found out that my uncle, who lived later in Paris, went to Auschwitz and that he survived.
So-- and from the books, because all of a sudden, I started reading like-- like a mad-- like a madperson about these concentration camps, and about the horrors, and about the thing.
And at that time, we found out everything.
As far as the Jewish life-- well, the few Jewish people that were left were really concentrating on my-- my aunt's house because she opened the hotel.
And whoever was coming really knew her, I mean, that came to the hotel and stayed.
So we heard stories from all over the place and from everybody.
And--
To me?
Well, I really didn't have much to do with the Poles, except I went to school in Lodz.
And I-- I went as Eva Najnudel.
Under my name, under-- as a Jewish girl.
Yeah, right.
As a Jewish girl.
And I came into class, and all of a sudden, there were a few other Jewish kids there in class.
And the Poles started picking up on them.
[POLISH] and [POLISH] and all kinds of names.
And it really got to me.
So all of a sudden, I-- I decided that I'm not going to be a Jew, because I don't want anybody picking on me.
So I started going to religion, to Catholic religion, and I started pray in class, and I tried to behave-- not with my name.
It was hard, but I did it anyhow.
Until Rosh Hashanah.
My aunt was very religious.
And for Rosh Hashanah, she said, you're not going to school today.
So I didn't.
Well, when I came back to school the next day, my teacher says to me, and where are you yesterday?
And I said to her, well, I was sick.
I wasn't feeling well.
And in front of the whole class, she says, well, wasn't that the Jewish holiday yesterday?
I said, yes.
So that was it.
So when we-- when we left Lodz and we moved to Legnica, it was again-- I went in as a Jew, and, you know-- and again, the same thing happened there.
There were a few Jewish kids in the class.
And again, they were called names and, you know, picked and whatever.
And I again looked at that.
And as much as I wanted to be myself, I said, I can't take it.
So I started again going to church.
I started going to religion.
I started being ashamed I was Jewish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and-- until-- that was from 1945 till 1951.
No, till 19-- yeah, till 1951 I was denying Jewishness.

When we had to register to get our papers in Poland, to get the document, like a passport-type of a document, the same like in Russia, I pressured my mother to change her parents' names so the passports-- so the documents are not going to be sounding too Jewish.
Well, that we were observing Catholic holidays.
I mean--
Yes, because I made everybody.
I put a lot of pressure on everybody.
Now, my stepfather was a very assimilated person.
I mean, he was-- religion was very, very far from-- you know, from whatever.
I mean, he did not-- he came from a very secular home.
They were very assimilated.
His parents were very assimilated.
Engelberg.
Now, but I put pressure on-- on everybody, and I stopped associating with Jewish kids.
I-- I was only associating with Poles.
And that was until 1951.
My mother was really pressuring me into, you know, not going out with Poles.
And she said, you know, you-- and she was-- there was a friend of my stepfather who converted way when, before the war, and they were giving me her as a example.
You see, she converted, so Jew didn't want her, and the Pole didn't want her.
So that's why she stayed single.
So-- so-- you know, so she said, you see?
And she was telling me the same story, this woman.
So my mother says, you see, if you do this, you know, nobody will want you and da, da, da, da.
And, you know, she was-- they were really brainwashing me.
But I couldn't break with it as long as I was in that town, you know?
Because I felt ashamed that I was putting up-- whatever.
So I said, OK, when I go to the university, to Bratslav, I'm going to cut with all this junk.
And sure enough, I-- when I went to the university, to Bratslav, I-- I dropped all my friends, all my Polish friends at that time.
And I got into a Jewish circle of friends and became, you know, very friendly.
We are still very friendly with-- with all of them.
Three years.
Three--
Four years.
Yeah, because I went to the university there.
From 1950-- no, I was there longer.
I'm sorry.
I was there from 1951 to 1956.
[INTERPOSING VOICES]
No, I think he survived as a Jew.
Yeah.
That was right after liberation, which was 19-- 1944.
I don't-- somebody must have taken me with horses and a buggy, or I walked.
Because out there, what we did-- I mean, the distances were quite-- you know, and we had no transportation, so we walked.
We would go into the forest and start walking-- you know, we were walking.
Sometimes, if a peasant would, you know, go by on the-- with a horse and a buggy, he would stop and take us.
And he would drop us off wherever he would turn, and we would walk again.
And that's how we would get places.
I don't remember if I walked, or somebody, you know, somebody took me.
I think I went alone.
I-- I don't think my mother and my sister went with me.
That was my private visit.
Well, by train, it's about an hour and a half to two hours.
Legnica?
Well, now, it's about 100,000 people.
